content.problemStatement:
We know that a sequence of NNN integers is called a permutation if it contains all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.

A K−K-K− beautiful permutation is a permutation for which KKK is the **minimum** number of good consecutive segments into which the sequence can be partitioned.

A good consecutive segment is a non-empty continuous segment where all elements of the segment have the same parity.

For example, the permutation [1,2,4,3][1, 2, 4, 3][1,2,4,3] can be divided into **minimum** 333 segments {[1],[2,4],[3]}\{ [1], [2, 4], [3] \}{[1],[2,4],[3]} where in each segment, all elements have same parity. Thus, this is a 3−3-3− beautiful permutation. Note that you can not divide [1,2,4,3][1, 2, 4, 3][1,2,4,3] into less than 333 consecutive segments which satisfy the given condition.

Chef had a permutation of size NNN which was K−K-K− beautiful but he forgot the elements at some of the positions of the permutation. Find the number of **distinct** permutations of length NNN that are K−K-K− beautiful and can be generated by filling the remaining positions of Chef's permutation.

Since the answer can be huge, print it modulo (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers P1,P2,…,PnP_1,P_2,\ldots,P_nP1​,P2​,…,Pn​ — the elements of the K−K-K− beautiful permutation. Here, Pi=0P_i = 0Pi​=0 means Chef forgot the element at ithi^{th}ith position.


Output Format
For each test case, print the number of **distinct** permutations of length NNN that are K−K-K− beautiful and can be generated by filling the remaining positions of Chef's permutation.


Since the answer can be huge, print it modulo (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7).
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 100100100.
